notes: Transformed for the initial atomref v0.1 scaffold.
Rahm 2016 is reclassified from van_der_waals to atomic_radius.

covalent_radius.description: Element-indexed covalent radii intended for geometry and bonding heuristics.

van_der_waals_radius.description: Element-indexed condensed-phase or contact-derived van der Waals radii.

atomic_radius.description: Element-indexed isolated-atom or theory-defined atomic radii used as transferable support data.

cordero2008.name: Cordero et al. covalent radii
cordero2008.description: Covalent radii from Cordero et al. (2008) (last author: Alvarez).
cordero2008.method_summary: Derived from crystallographic bond distances (primarily single bonds) across the periodic table.
cordero2008.extraction_source: Table 2 in B. Cordero et al. (2008), column 'r'
notes: The source paper provides multiple radii per element for different atom types/environments; this package currently includes C(sp3) value for C and high-spin values for Mn/Fe/Co.

csd_legacy_cov.name: CSD legacy covalent radii (bond perception)
csd_legacy_cov.description: Legacy covalent radii used in CSD software for bond assignment (Rcov).
csd_legacy_cov.extraction_source: CCDC Elemental_Radii.xlsx (CSD radii table), column 'Covalent Radius'.
notes: CSD bond assignment heuristic: a bond A-B may be inferred if distance d satisfies Rcov(A)+Rcov(B)-t <= d <= Rcov(A)+Rcov(B)+t, with typical t=0.4 Å. (See the CCDC spreadsheet notes.)
For Z>=111, csd_legacy values are omitted because the legacy CSD table does not provide radii beyond Darmstadtium (Z=110).
Elements not yet encountered in the CSD have Rcov = 1.50 Å.

bondi1964.name: Bondi van der Waals radii
bondi1964.description: Classic van der Waals radii compiled by Bondi (1964), available for 38 elements.
bondi1964.method_summary: Bondi compiled van der Waals radii from a combination of experimental sources (e.g., crystal structures, liquid-state properties, gas kinetic data) to reproduce molecular/atomic volumes and sizes. This set is widely used as a historical reference and in many computational chemistry defaults.
bondi1964.extraction_source: Bondi column in Table 1 of Alvarez (2013) (used as a convenient transcription of Bondi's tabulation).

references.title: van der Waals Volumes and Radii
A cartography of the van der Waals territories
references.note: Table 1 reproduces Bondi radii for 38 elements.

notes: Coverage is limited (38 elements, including only a few transition metals and uranium).
Because Bondi radii were not derived exclusively from crystal nonbonded contact statistics, they can differ slightly from later 'structural' vdW radii.

rowland_taylor1996.name: Rowland & Taylor nonbonded contact radii
rowland_taylor1996.description: Nonbonded contact radii derived from organic crystal structures (Rowland & Taylor, 1996).
rowland_taylor1996.method_summary: Rowland & Taylor analyzed distributions of intermolecular nonbonded contact distances in organic crystal structures from the Cambridge Structural Database (CSD). They fitted/estimated characteristic contact distances and solved for per-element radii by least-squares analysis over many element-pair distance distributions.
rowland_taylor1996.extraction_source: Table 3 in Rowland & Taylor (1996), column 'r_c' (least-squares radii, not the normalized R_d column).

references.title: Intermolecular Nonbonded Contact Distances in Organic Crystal Structures: Comparison with Distances Expected from van der Waals Radii

notes: Coverage is intentionally limited to common organic-crystal nonmetals (H, C, N, O, F, S, Cl, Br, I).
Rowland & Taylor also report a normalized set (R_d) constrained to match the total of Bondi radii; this package uses the raw least-squares r_c values.

alvarez2013.name: Alvarez van der Waals radii
alvarez2013.description: van der Waals radii from Alvarez (2013).

references.title: A cartography of the van der Waals territories

notes: Obtained by statistical analysis of millions of interatomic distances in the Cambridge Structural Database (CSD), locating the vdW peak after the vdW gap.

chernyshov2020.name: Chernyshov LoS van der Waals radii
chernyshov2020.description: van der Waals radii from Chernyshov et al. (ChemPhysChem 2020) using line-of-sight (LoS) classification of direct contacts.
chernyshov2020.method_summary: Chernyshov et al. introduce a line-of-sight (LoS) criterion to identify 'direct' interatomic contacts in complex molecular crystals. vdW radii are then inferred from statistically analyzed contact-distance distributions for specific atom types, yielding radii (including R_half and R_max variants) intended to better reflect steric/anistropic effects than simple distance-based heuristics.
chernyshov2020.extraction_source: Table 1 in Chernyshov et al. (2020): R_max values for the 'default' atom types typical for organic compounds.

references.authors: I. Yu. Chernyshov; I. V. Ananyev; E. A. Pidko
references.title: Revisiting van der Waals Radii: From Comprehensive Structural Analysis to Knowledge-Based Classification of Interatomic Contacts

notes: The source paper provides multiple radii per element for different atom types/environments; this package currently includes only the main/default R_max values used in Table 1.
Primarily targeted at elements common in organic crystals (H, C, N, O, F, S, Cl, Se, Br, I).

csd_legacy_vdw.name: CSD legacy van der Waals radii (pre-2024.3)
csd_legacy_vdw.description: Legacy van der Waals radii historically used in CSD tools (pre-2024.3).
csd_legacy_vdw.extraction_source: CCDC Elemental_Radii.xlsx (CSD radii table), column 'vdW Radius' (Bondi/Rowland-Taylor based with defaults).

references.title: van der Waals Volumes and Radii
Intermolecular Nonbonded Contact Distances in Organic Crystal Structures: Comparison with Distances Expected from van der Waals Radii
Updates to van der Waals radii used in the CSD and Mercury

notes: For Z>=111, csd_legacy values are omitted because the legacy CSD table does not provide radii beyond Darmstadtium (Z=110).
Radii that are not available in either Bondi or Rowland & Taylor versions were assigned RvdW of 2.00 Å.
The CSD 2024.3 release updated the vdW radii used in CSD and Mercury to Alvarez-derived values (see CCDC blog post).

rahm2016.name: Rahm isodensity atomic radii (ρ=0.001 e/bohr³)
rahm2016.description: Computed atomic radii for neutral atoms (elements 1–96) defined by the ρ=0.001 e/bohr³ electron-density isosurface (Rahm et al., 2016).
rahm2016.method_summary: Rahm et al. computed relativistic all-electron DFT electron densities (close to the basis-set limit) for isolated atoms and ions. Radii are defined by an electron-density threshold, producing a consistent, theory-based size measure that correlates well with structural van der Waals radii derived from crystal structures.
rahm2016.extraction_source: Supporting Information for Rahm et al. (2016), Table S1: neutral-atom radii for elements 1–96.

references.authors: M. Rahm; R. Hoffmann; N. W. Ashcroft
references.title: Atomic and Ionic Radii of Elements 1–96
Chem. Eur. J. 2016, 22, 14625–14632 (Rahm et al.) – Misc. Information
references.note: Table S1 contains the neutral-atom radii used here.

notes: The original work also reports cationic radii (+1) for the first 96 elements and selected anionic radii (−1) for some elements; these are not yet included in the current CSV.

In atomref this dataset is classified as atomic support data, not as a direct condensed-phase van der Waals-radius set, because it describes isolated atoms in vacuum and is used here primarily as a transferable baseline.
